expectations.text: A single TDD-*.md file exists in the outputs/ directory
expectations.evidence: TDD-order-projections-service.md exists in outputs/ directory

expectations.text: Document is at least 1500 words
expectations.evidence: Document is substantially longer than 1500 words, with 17 detailed sections including extensive prose, tables, and code blocks

expectations.text: Contains a 'Problem' / 'Context' / 'Background' section
expectations.evidence: Section 2 'Problem statement & scope' and Section 3 'Background and constraints (calibrated)'

expectations.text: Contains a 'Scope' section with both in-scope and out-of-scope items
expectations.evidence: Section 2.1 'What we are building' and 2.2 'What we are explicitly not building (this milestone)' enumerate both

expectations.text: Contains a 'High-level approach' / 'Overview' section
expectations.evidence: Section 5 'High-level architecture' with full diagram and component breakdown

expectations.text: Contains a 'Detailed design' section with substantive content
expectations.evidence: Sections 6 'Consumer design' and 7 'Storage' provide deeply substantive detail including reducer model, CAS write pseudocode, schema DDL

expectations.text: Contains 'Alternatives considered' with at least 2 alternatives and rejection rationale for each
expectations.evidence: Section 7.2 table compares DynamoDB, Spanner, Cloud SQL, Firestore, Bigtable, Redis with rejection rationale for each

expectations.text: Contains an NFRs section with concrete targets (numbers/SLOs)
expectations.evidence: Section 4.2 lists NFR-1 read p99 < 50ms, NFR-2 freshness p95 < 2s, NFR-3 99.95% availability, NFR-6 backfill ≤ 2h

expectations.text: Contains a 'Risks' / 'Failure modes' section
expectations.evidence: Section 12 'Failure modes and how we handle them' with detection/behavior/recovery table

expectations.text: Contains a 'Rollout' / 'Migration' section
expectations.evidence: Section 15 'Rollout plan' with 16-week milestone schedule and 15.1 'Rollback'

expectations.text: Contains an 'Observability' / 'Operations' section
expectations.evidence: Section 14 'Observability' with metrics, tracing, logs, SLOs, dashboards subsections

expectations.text: Contains 'Open questions' with at least one entry
expectations.evidence: Section 16 'Open questions and dependencies' lists 11 specific entries including sequence field, producer idempotency, retention, PII

expectations.text: No fabricated/invented URLs to internal systems
expectations.evidence: No invented internal URLs; only the actual API path /orders/{order_id}/projection and admin endpoint are mentioned

expectations.text: TDD is structured as an event-driven / streaming consumer design
expectations.evidence: Designed around Kafka topic consumption with one consumer group, partition workers, DLQ topic, and reducer over event stream

expectations.text: Explicitly states delivery semantics (at-least-once + idempotent processing) and what makes effects idempotent
expectations.evidence: Section 3 states 'Delivery semantics: at-least-once. Duplicates are guaranteed; the consumer must be idempotent.' Section 6.3 details event_id dedup + last_applied_sequence CAS as idempotency mechanisms

expectations.text: Discusses per-partition ordering and partition key choice (order_id) and what that buys
expectations.evidence: Section 3 notes 'topic is keyed by order_id and a single key always lands on a single partition; Kafka guarantees per-partition order.' Section 6.4 enforces one worker per partition

expectations.text: Specifies a consumer lag SLO with a concrete number
expectations.evidence: NFR-2 freshness p95 < 2s with alert at 1.5s; NFR-3 'consumer lag < 30s' availability condition

expectations.text: Specifies a DLQ for bad transitions / poison messages with trigger and replay path
expectations.evidence: Section 6.6 defines orders.events.dlq.v1 with reason taxonomy (invalid_transition, sequence_gap_timeout, cas_storm, decode_error, unknown_event_type); replay path via §8 tool

expectations.text: Specifies offset commit strategy (after processing / batch / at-least-once semantics)
expectations.evidence: Section 6.7 'enable.auto.commit=false; we commit synchronously after successful store write'; Section 6.3 step 6 'Commit the Kafka offset only after the write succeeds'

expectations.text: Takes an actual position on storage (DynamoDB vs Postgres vs Redis) with stated tradeoffs, not punted
expectations.evidence: Section 7.3 explicitly recommends Cloud Spanner as primary, rejects DynamoDB (cross-cloud), and identifies Cloud SQL Postgres as fallback if cost vetoed

expectations.text: Addresses Avro schema evolution under BACKWARDS compatibility
expectations.evidence: Section 11 'Schema evolution': 'Input events: governed by Confluent SR with BACKWARDS compat. The consumer is built against the latest schema; old events deserialize cleanly because new fields are optional with defaults.'

expectations.text: Addresses out-of-order event handling within partition (sequence numbers / event-timestamp last-writer-wins / state-machine guards)
expectations.evidence: Section 6.2 mandates per-order_id monotonic sequence number; Section 6.3 uses sequence for staleness drop and CAS guard; Section 6.5 introduces park buffer for gaps

expectations.text: Designs back-fill / full-replay path for initial seed and recovery
expectations.evidence: Section 8 covers single-order replay (8.1) and full backfill (8.2) with shadow-table approach and 2h throughput target

expectations.text: Designs the GET /orders/{id}/projection read endpoint with latency target
expectations.evidence: Section 9 specifies the endpoint contract with auth, JSON response shape, 404/503 semantics, staleness headers; NFR-1 sets p99 < 50ms latency target
